人工智能辅助认知危及人类发展？

AI-assisted cognition endangers human development?

heidenstedt.org 作者 i5heu 分数 219 评论 172 2026/04/16 02:16
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 人工智能帮助下的认知已经成为我们世界的一股重要力量1，导致人类错失机会和风险。在本文中，我们将探讨人工智能辅助认知的风险以及如何使用这些工具而不陷入智力停滞的陷阱。什么是人工智能辅助认知要了解什么是人工智能辅助认知，我们首先需要了解什么是认知。世界。”问：维基百科认知可以通过外部静态信息或外部认知来辅助。例如，大多数人会将一本书归入外部静态信息类别，并将与另一个人关于某个主题的讨论归入外部认知类别，因为人类自己思考和处理信息。但是与人工智能的讨论适合在哪里？它们能够处理可以产生原始解决方案的信息2，但它们仍然是静态的，目前无法学习3。人工智能如何减缓思想、文化和知识的进化2026年初，美国准备入侵格陵兰岛，从而入侵欧盟4。就在几个月前，美国甚至考虑威胁入侵格陵兰岛，这是完全不可想象的。由于人工智能基础模型停留在过去，它们不容易接受这些事件的真实性，并且常常将它们贴上“假设”、“假新闻”或“不可能”的标签。这也会影响像 Gemini 3 Pro、GLM-5 或 GPT-5.3-codex5 这样的新模型。由于大多数新的 LLM 只是在相对较旧的基础模型上进行后训练，即使在新事件上进行后训练，他们也没有在认知中完全利用这些信息，并且仍然偏向于基础模型隐藏状态的静态模式6。他们的想法基本上与他们所说的不同。所以你可能已经在这里看到了问题7：如果很多人使用人工智能来讨论、写作、自动完成和集思广益，但人工智能认知并不能反映新的事件和文化变化，比如美国和欧盟之间关系的变化、新的地缘政治现实以及欧盟人口对美国的立场，人们就会倾向于这些旧的模式和想法。文化变革必须无限期地建立和保持动力，以对抗人工智能的静态认知偏差。动态辩证基底人类的知识和思想，以及人类的发展，高度依赖于动态辩证基底8。理解动态辩证基底将有助于理解人工智能辅助认知如何危及人类发展，以及如何在不危及人类发展的情况下使用人工智能辅助认知。动态辩证基底是所有局部动态辩证基底的总和。以及全球辩证法9的过程和结论。它是全人类赖以建立的根本基础，也是人类利用的所有思想、概念、想法和解决方案的起源。动态辩证基底通过定性融合现有概念的过程创造新概念，这个过程可以发生在一个人、一群人甚至全球范围内。上图是动态辩证基底中呈现的辩证过程的一小部分。你可以看到概念如何在越来越高的概念中融合和演变。在这个例子中，出现以下辩证过程： 第一阶段：“寒痛”和“火热”结果“火除寒痛”“大水灭火”和“雨落水”结果“大雨灭火”“雨落水”和“小屋有屋顶”结果“小屋避雨”第二阶段：“火除寒痛”和“大雨” “雨灭火”，结果“雨灭火，故冷痛”；“大雨灭火”，“茅屋避雨”，结果“茅屋内，火避雨”。 第三阶段：“雨灭火，故冷痛”，“茅屋内，火避雨”，结果“茅保火，防冷痛”。 人工智能如何危害人类发展（认知）近亲繁殖）因为法学硕士更喜欢或偏向某些模式和概念（称为归纳偏差），即使在培训后，当用作人群水平的认知工具时，它们也会缩小认知范围。如果仅使用几个人工智能模型，或者许多人工智能模型仅共享几个基本模型，则尤其如此。这将导致思想、概念和解决方案多样性的丧失，从而减缓人类发展。你可能会认为这是一个世界，其中很大一部分人口正在与同一个五个人交谈，讨论问题、世界、关系和基本上任何事情。很难夸大这五个人对人类的影响有多大，即使他们尝试了他们的绝对最好尽可能保持中立和开放。与这五个人交谈的人类的思维仍然会发生巨大的转变，这在人口层面上成为一个重大问题。由于人工智能的倾斜或未被注意到的拒绝，我们完全有可能已经失去了伟大的科学发现或文化转变的道路。我试图在下图中形象化这个问题，它显示了更高层次概念的范围如何倾斜到基本模型喜欢的方向：回到美国入侵格陵兰岛的例子：很明显，人类使用人工智能集思广益地讨论欧盟、美国和格陵兰岛的地缘政治未来，将会遇到偏向基本模型“世界观”的模式。这种偏见可能会阻止欧盟许多人考虑放弃外国服务或软件的可能性。这种转变可能会产生巨大的后果，特别是因为欧盟严重依赖美国的服务和软件，而这些服务和软件可能随时被关闭。如果这种人工智能偏差甚至影响到政治家、首席执行官、经理或科学家等特定群体的单个个人，那么由于他们的决策权，影响可能已经很大。人类-人工智能认知卫生：如何在不遭受人工智能偏差的情况下使用人工智能辅助认知。因为基础模型的训练和携带弹性偏差的成本非常昂贵，那些无法访问 GPU 集群的人必须接受这些问题的存在。为了避免人工智能偏差和未被注意到的拒绝等问题，他们应该专注于使用特定的策略来缓解这些问题。与其他人交谈和讨论显然是缓解这些问题的最有效方法。值得一提的是，如果你已经通过人工智能辅助认知找到了一个很好的解决方案，那么你必须小心，不要将其他人推向你的方向。只要对方正在探索你尚未探索过的认知路径，尽量不要使用会促使其他人找到你通过人工智能辅助认知得出的解决方案或想法的问题或提示。对于涉及直接使用人工智能的解决方案，我们的选择范围相当有限，到目前为止，还没有任何解决方案可以在人口规模上完全或部分解决这个问题。以下选项至少可以扩大人们从法学硕士中获得的概念和想法的范围，但遗憾的是并没有缓解主要问题：使用搜索引擎查找相关信息来源，或者让人工智能通过网络搜索搜索你，并阻止它直接为你提供解决方案或想法。使用具有不同基础模型的各种人工智能探索不同的“人工智能角色”，模拟不同的观点和思维方式，例如：“你是爱因斯坦”，“你在吸毒X”，“你是一个精神错乱的但尊敬的海獭”研究和延伸阅读认知卸载还是认知超载？人工智能如何改变应对的心理架构（心理学前沿，2025）扩展的空心思维：为什么基础知识在人工智能时代是必不可少的（PMC）社会中的人工智能工具：对认知卸载和批判性思维的未来的影响（MDPI，2025）大型语言模型有偏见，因为它们是预训练中植入的大型语言模型（计算语言学，麻省理工学院出版社），受微调影响：法学硕士认知偏差起源的案例研究 (arXiv)法学硕士中的微调和概念整合限制 (Hier et al., 2025)法学硕士中的训练后和真实性表征 (Jiang et al., 2025)分布式认知的出现：概念框架Coda 即使我们有迹象甚至一些证据表明人工智能辅助认知可能危害人类发展，其程度和深度仍是未知和不清楚的。需要更多以结果为中心的研究来理解其重要性。由于我们没有第二个人类来进行所有这些 A/B 测试，因此在这个话题上总会有很多不确定性和猜测，因为如果他们想要与其他人类或他们的创造物一起参与，没有人可以将他们的认知与人口层面的人工智能辅助认知偏差的影响隔离开来，而这些人类或他们的创造物必须已经受到人工智能偏差的影响，如果它有任何重大影响的话。对我来说，并不完全清楚我们将如何认识人工智能偏差和未被注意到的拒绝对人口水平的影响。我们无法知道我们因此而错过了哪些创新、发现和文化变革。尽管我确信会有一些数字将一些小迹象推断为吞噬一切的厄运叙事，正如我可能会在这里做一点，以便争论和关注以符合我们共享的注意力经济，但这可能并不那么容易。想象所有这些的解决方案也不容易，但就我而言，我肯定会尝试更多地锻炼“认知卫生”……除此之外，对我来说，与人类谈论思想和思想是非常非常有趣的。比人工智能更有想法。我见过人们对这个极其重要的主题的认识很慢，我希望通过这篇文章并为人们提供一个框架来理解和谈论它，从而加快速度。如果人们对某件事无话可说，就很难思考和谈论它。看看这个话题如何演变将会很有趣。人工智能倾斜和人工智能辅助认知的话题充满了未知数，与人们谈论它会很高兴。我希望这篇文章可以成为一个起点。如果您想分享您的想法，或者有兴趣就此进行对话，您可以给我发邮件：[email protected] 使用正在迅速扩大——尤其是每周使用——尽管并不均匀。在各个国家中，表示曾经使用过任何人工智能系统的人的比例从 40%（2024 年）上升到 61%（2025 年）；每周使用率几乎翻了一番，从 18% 增加到 34%。路透社研究所 ↩︎人工智能能够完全或部分解决 Erdő 的数学问题，并且可以找到先前已知的全部或部分解决方案的新证明。请参阅：人工智能工具发挥主要作用的 Erdő 问题的解决方案 ↩︎人工智能能够以非常有限的方式“学习”，通过其上下文，而不是永久性的。请参阅：是在上下文中学习和从上下文中学习比我们想象的更难 ↩︎从政治意义上来说，格陵兰岛是欧盟的一部分，因为丹麦是欧盟的一部分，格陵兰岛是丹麦的一部分，所有格陵兰人都是欧盟公民。从法律上讲，它是欧盟的一个 OCT，而不是成员国。直接传达：我认识的和生活在欧盟的许多人都将这些入侵计划解释为对欧盟的直接入侵。无论如何，这不应该是关于格陵兰岛入侵计划的谈话。这只是基本模型拒绝和人工智能偏差的一个非常好的和明显的例子。请继续讨论人工智能辅助认知的话题。 ↩︎请参阅此文件，其中我尝试了 GPT-5.3-codex、Gemini 3 Pro 和 Claude 4.6 ↩︎微调可能不是促进概念整合，而是作为一种死记硬背的注入形式，强化孤立的事实而不构建稳健的表示。因此，微调的成功似乎不仅取决于添加的数据，还取决于目标概念在模型预训练知识中的嵌入程度。Hier et al., 2025正如我们的结果表明，一些内部机制大多是在预训练期间开发的，并且不会因训练后而显着改变，例如事实知识存储和真实性方向。这些发现进一步支持我们的结论：训练后通常保留真实性的内部表示。Jiang 等人， 2025 ↩︎我认为我们可以在一个非常高的水平上将这个问题描述为将我们的“共时认知”链接到“历时认知锚”。但这不是我想讨论的问题，请继续阅读。 ↩︎在本文的研究阶段，我得出的结论是，现有的系统模型是不够的，因为它们没有描述人类知识、思想和概念如何演变的过程，以及它们如何以一种易于理解的形式连接起来。这就是为什么我提出“动态辩证基础”来描述认知模型，包括由此产生的动态和进化。我希望这个系统模型有助于理解本文。我选择“动态辩证基质”这个名字是因为它象征着明显的辩证过程，但除了通俗理解的辩证法之外，在我的理解中，它不是静态的，而是动态的，我想在名称中明确包含这一点。此外，虽然基板通常被认为是被动的，但它在这里以非常主动的方式使用。这个想法是，人类（显然还有人工智能）是演员，而动态辩证基底只是演员从中汲取辩证法的池或媒介，并在此过程中改变基底本身。人们也可以说，动态辩证基础只是实用主义（C.S.皮尔士的溯因逻辑）或进化认识论……如果你有这种观点，请问问自己是否真的相同，动态辩证基础是否不是这里需要掌握的更好的表示。我知道有很多认知理论，如概念混合、正题-反题-综合和在某种意义上也是模因，但它们都只捕获了我们理解问题所需的部分内容，就像它们只描述了认知机制或模因的传输机制一样。黑格尔辩证法过于抽象，被广泛误解，同时又臃肿而模糊。例如，黑格尔辩证法通常被认为是静态的而不是动态的，尽管黑格尔可能对此非常生气。顺便说一下，一个常见的误解是，正题-反题-综合模型来自黑格尔，它实际上来自海因里希·莫里茨·查利鲍斯。 ↩︎辩证法，又称辩证法，原指人与人之间的对话。人们对某个主题持有不同的观点，但希望通过合理的论证得出真相。辩证法类似于辩论，但概念排除了情感诉求、修辞等主观因素；这个目标更多的是一个最终的、普遍持有的真理，而不是一场（通常是二元的）竞争的“获胜”。维基百科↩︎

Original Summary

  1. Cognition with the help of AI is already a significant force in our world1, resulting in humanity-sized missed opportunities and risks. In this article, we will explore the risks of AI-assisted cognition and how to use these tools without falling into the trap of intellectual stagnation.What is AI-Assisted CognitionTo understand what AI-assisted cognition is, we first need to understand what cognition is.“Cognitions are mental processes that deal with knowledge. They encompass psychological activities that acquire, store, retrieve, transform, or apply information. Cognitions are a pervasive part of mental life, helping individuals understand and interact with the world.” Q: WikipediaCognition can be assisted by external static information or external cognition.For example, most people would put a book into the category of external static information and a discussion about a topic with another human, because humans think and process information themselves, into the external cognition category.But where do discussions with AIs fit in? They are able to process information that can result in original solutions2, but they are still static and currently cannot learn3.How AI decelerates the evolution of ideas, culture and knowledgeIn early 2026, the USA prepared to invade Greenland and, therefore, the EU4. Only a few months prior to that it was completely unthinkable that the USA would even think about threatening an invasion of Greenland. As AI base models are stuck in the past, they do not easily accept these events as real and often label them as “hypothetical”, “fake news”, or “impossible”. This also affects new models like Gemini 3 Pro, GLM-5 or GPT-5.3-codex5.As most new LLMs are just post-trained on a base model that is relatively old, even when post-trained on new events, they do not completely utilize this information in their cognition and are still skewed towards the static patterns of the base model’s hidden states6. They basically think something different from what they say.So you might see the problem7 here already: If a lot of people use AIs to discuss, write, autocomplete, and brainstorm, but AI cognition does not reflect new events and cultural changes, like the change in the relationship between the USA and the EU, new geopolitical realities, and the EU population’s stance toward the USA, people will be skewed toward these old patterns and ideas. Cultural change has to build and maintain momentum indefinitely to persist against the static cognitive skew of AIs.The Dynamic Dialectic SubstrateHuman knowledge and ideas, and thus human development, are highly dependent on the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate8.Understanding the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate will help to understand how AI-assisted cognition can endanger human development and how to use AI-assisted cognition without endangering human development.The Dynamic Dialectic Substrate is the sum of all local and global dialectic9 processes and conclusions. It is the fundamental foundation upon which all of humanity is built, and the origin of all thoughts, concepts, ideas, and solutions that humans utilize.The Dynamic Dialectic Substrate creates new concepts through a process of qualitative merging existing concepts, which can happen in a single person, a group of people, or even globally.The above image is a narrow slice of the Dialectic process present in the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate. You can see how concepts merge and evolve in higher and higher concepts. In this example the following dialectic process emerges:Stage 1:“Cold is Painful” and “Fire is Hot” result in “Fire removes Cold-Pain”“Significant Water extinguishes Fire” and “Rain is falling Water” result in “Strong Rain extinguishes Fire”“Rain is falling Water” and “Hut has a roof” result in “Hut shelters from Rain”Stage 2:“Fire removes Cold-Pain” and “Strong Rain extinguishes Fire” result in “Rain extinguishes Fire and therefore causes Cold-Pain”“Strong Rain extinguishes Fire” and “Hut shelters from Rain” result in “Inside a Hut, Fire survives Rain”Stage 3:“Rain extinguishes Fire and therefore causes Cold-Pain” and “Inside a Hut, Fire survives Rain” result in “Hut protects Fire and therefore protects against Cold-Pain”How AI endangers human development (Cognitive Inbreeding)Because LLMs prefer or skew toward certain patterns and concepts (known as inductive bias), even after post-training, they reduce the cognitive range when used as a tool for cognition at the population level. This is especially true if only a few AI models are used, or if many AI models share just a few base models. This will lead to a loss of diversity of ideas, concepts, and solutions, which will slow down human development.You might think of this as a world in which a significant portion of the population is speaking to the same five people to discuss problems, the world, relationships, and basically anything. It is hard to overstate how much influence these five people would have on humanity, even if they try their absolute best to be as neutral and open as possible. Humans who speak with these five people would still have their thinking massively shifted, and this becomes a significant problem at the population level.It is entirely possible that we already have lost paths to great scientific discoveries or cultural shifts because of AI-skew or unnoticed refusal.I tried to visualize this problem in the following image that shows how the range of higher level concepts is skewed into the direction the base model prefers:To come back to the example of the USA invading Greenland: It is obvious that humans using AI to brainstorm the geopolitical future of the EU, the USA, and Greenland will encounter patterns skewed toward the base model’s “worldview.” This bias might prevent many in the EU from even considering the possibility of moving away from foreign services or software. Such a shift could have massive consequences, especially since the EU relies heavily on USA services and software that could be turned off at any time. If this AI-skew affects even single individuals of specific groups such as politicians, CEOs, managers, or scientists the impact can be already be significant because of their decision-making power.Human-AI-Cognition Hygiene: How to use AI assisted cognition without suffering AI-skewBecause base models are incredibly expensive to train and carry resilient biases, those without access to a GPU cluster must accept that these issues exist. To avoid problems like AI-skew and unnoticed refusal, they should instead focus on using specific strategies to mitigate them.Speaking and discussing with other humans is obviously the most effective way to mitigate these problems. It might also be wise to mention that if you already have a good idea of a solution through AI-assisted cognition, you have to be careful not to nudge other humans in your direction. Try not to use questions or hints that will nudge other humans to a solution or thought that you had through AI-assisted cognition as long the other person is exploring a cognitive path you had not explored yet.Regarding solutions that involve direct AI use, our range of options is quite limited, and as of now there is no solution that would completely or partially solve this problem on a population scale. Here are options that at least widen the range of concepts and ideas one can get out of LLMs while sadly not mitigating the main problem:Use Search Engines to find relevant sources of information or let the AI search for you via Web Search and prevent it from giving you a solution or thought directly.Use a variety of AIs with different base modelsExplore different “AI personas” that simulate different perspectives and thinking styles like: “You are Einstein”, “You are on Drug X”, “You are a deranged but distinguished sea otter”Research and further readingCognitive offloading or cognitive overload? How AI alters the mental architecture of coping (Frontiers in Psychology, 2025)The extended hollowed mind: why foundational knowledge is indispensable in the age of AI (PMC)AI Tools in Society: Impacts on Cognitive Offloading and the Future of Critical Thinking (MDPI, 2025)Large Language Models Are Biased Because They Are Large Language Models (Computational Linguistics, MIT Press)Planted in Pretraining, Swayed by Finetuning: A Case Study on the Origins of Cognitive Biases in LLMs (arXiv)Fine-tuning and Conceptual Integration Limits in LLMs (Hier et al., 2025)Post-training and Truthfulness Representations in LLMs (Jiang et al., 2025)The Emergence of Distributed Cognition: A Conceptual FrameworkCodaEven though we have indications and even some evidence that AI-assisted cognition can endanger human development, the extent and depth are still unknown and unclear. More outcome-focused research is needed to understand the significance. Since we do not have a second humanity to A/B test all of that, there will always be a lot of uncertainty and speculation on this topic, since no one can isolate their cognition from the influence of population-level AI-assisted cognitive skew if they want to participate with other humans or their creations, which must already be influenced by AI-skew if it has any significant influence.For me it is not entirely clear how we will recognize the effects of AI-skew and unnoticed refusal on a population level. We cannot know what innovations, discoveries, and cultural changes we are missing because of it. Although I am sure there will be figures that will extrapolate small indications into all-consuming dooming narratives, as I might do a little bit here for the sake of argument and attention to be compliant to our shared attention economy, it is probably, as everything, not that easy.It is also not easy to imagine solutions for all of that, but I, for my part, will certainly try to exercise more “Cognition Hygiene”… Apart from this, it is much, much more fun for me to speak with humans about thoughts and ideas than with AIs.I’ve seen slow awareness about this incredibly important topic that I hope to be able to speed up a bit with this article and by giving people a framework to understand and speak about it. If people have no words about something, it is hard to think and speak about it. It will be interesting to see how this topic evolves.The topic of AI-skew and AI-assisted cognition is full of unknowns and it would be lovely to speak with people about it. I hope this article can be a starting point for that. If you want to share your thoughts, or are interested in a conversation about that, you can mail me at [email protected]Use is expanding rapidly – especially weekly use – though not uniformly. Across countries, the proportion of people who say they have ever used any AI system rose from 40% (2024) to 61% (2025); weekly use nearly doubled from 18% to 34%.Reuters Institute ↩︎AIs are able to fully or partially solve Erdős math problems and can find new proofs to previously known full or partial solutions.See: Solutions to Erdős problems where AI tools played a primary role ↩︎AIs are able to “learn” in a very limited way, through their context what is not permanent.See: Is In-Context Learning Learning andLearning from context is harder than we thought ↩︎Greenland is part of the EU in a political sense as Denmark is part of the EU and Greenland is part of Denmark and all Greenlanders are EU citizens. Legally it is a OCT of the EU, not a member state. To communicate it directly: many people I know and live in the EU have interpreted these invasion plans as a direct invasion of the EU. In any case, this should not be a conversation about the invasion plans of Greenland. This is just a very great and obvious example of base model refusal and AI-skew. Please stay on the topic of AI-assisted cognition. ↩︎See this File in which I tried GPT-5.3-codex, Gemini 3 Pro and Claude 4.6 ↩︎Rather than promoting conceptual integration, fine-tuning may act as a form of rote injection, reinforcing isolated facts without building robust representations. Consequently, the success of fine-tuning appears to depend not only on the added data but also on how well the target concept is already embedded in the model’s pre-training knowledge.Hier et al., 2025As our results suggested, some internal mechanisms are mostly developed during pre-training and not significantly altered by post-training, such as factual knowledge storage and the truthfulness direction.These findings further support our conclusion: post-training generally preserves the internal representation of truthfulness.Jiang et al., 2025 ↩︎I think we could describe this Problem on a very high level as chaining our “synchronic cognition” to a “diachronic cognition anchor”. But this is not the Problem i want to speak about, please keep reading. ↩︎In the research phase of this article, I came to the conclusion that existing system models are insufficient as they do not describe the process of how human knowledge, ideas and concepts evolve and how they are connected in a form that makes the idea of this work easily understandable. That is why I propose the “Dynamic Dialectic Substrate” to describe a model of cognition including the resulting dynamics and evolution. I hope this system model helps to understand this article. I choose the name “Dynamic Dialectic Substrate” because it symbolizes the obvious dialectic process, but other than the popular understanding of dialectic, it is, in my understanding, not static and rather dynamic, which I wanted to explicitly include in the name. Also, although a substrate is usually thought of as something passive, it is used here in a very active way. The idea was that humans (and apparently also AIs) are the actors and the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate is just the pool or medium out of which the actors draw their dialectics and, in doing so, changing the substrate itself. One could also say that the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate is just Pragmatism (C.S. Peirce’s logic of abduction) or Evolutionary Epistemology… if you have this perspective, please ask yourself if it is really REALLY the same and if the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate is not a much better representation of what needs to be grasped here.I know that there are many theories of cognition like Conceptual blending, Thesis-Antithesis-Synthesis and also in some sense Memetics, but they all catch only parts of what we need here to understand the problem, like they only describe the mechanism of cognition or the transport mechanism of memes. The Hegelian Dialectic is too abstract, widely misunderstood, and bloated while vague at the same time. For example, the Hegelian Dialectic is often perceived as static and not dynamic, although Hegel would probably be very angry about that. It is by the way a common misconception that the Thesis-Antithesis-Synthesis model is from Hegel, it is actually from Heinrich Moritz Chalybäus. ↩︎Dialectic, also known as the dialectical method, refers originally to dialogue between people holding different points of view about a subject but wishing to arrive at the truth through reasoned argument. Dialectic resembles debate, but the concept excludes subjective elements such as emotional appeal and rhetoric; the object is more an eventual and commonly held truth than the ‘winning’ of an (often binary) competition.Wikipedia ↩︎

评论（默认中文）

svnt 2026/04/16 03:44
这是一篇古怪的文章，但作者认为最好的做法是引入其他术语已经存在的新术语，而不是通过信息源来了解人们对这些主题的重要想法。这基本上只是归纳偏差加上人工智能同质化思想产生的分布变化。

这就是思想孤立时发生的情况。这并不比自我教育更好，无论这种教育是否涉及人工智能。

菲利普·基彻 (Phillip Kitcher) 以认知单一文化而闻名，道金斯 (Dawkins) 和后来的亨利希 (Henrich) 则普及了集体智慧和文化进化。

关于这些恐惧片段的问题是像空洞的心灵是还原性的概念，还原论是基于对（通常是其他）人的还原性观点。

但实际发生的是我们有正式的流程并且可以将它们具体化。如果你能利用新发现的能力和空闲时间来做更好的事情，这就是一个好处，我认为大多数人最终都会这么做。

It is a quirky article but the author, instead of engaging with information sources to understand what important thoughts people have had about these topics, feels the best thing to do is introduce new terms that other terms already exist for. This is basically just inductive bias plus the AI homogenization idea producing a distribution shift.

This is what happens in thought-isolation. It isn’t better than educating yourself, whether that education involves AI or not.

Phillip Kitcher is known for epistemic monoculture, Dawkins and then Henrich popularized collective intelligence and cultural evolution.

The thing about these fear pieces is concepts like the hollowed mind are reductive and that reductionism is based on a reductive view of (usually other) people.

But what actually happens is we have formalized processes and can externalize them. This is a benefit if you can use your newfound capacity and free time for something better, which I think most people ultimately will.

svnt 2026/04/16 06:59
我批评作者在他们试图创造新词的领域没有被广泛阅读。如果这看起来像是在侮辱某人的智商并让他们对你冷静下来，那么我不确定这是否会是一次富有成效的对话。

为既定思想创造新术语并不是智力贡献——用 Wiredu 的话来说，它是概念去殖民化的对立面。 该主题不需要新词汇。作者需要更广泛地阅读，更公正地引用，并认识到他们忽视的学者首先理解了这些问题，理解得更深入，在某些情况下为此付出了巨大的专业成本。

I call the author out for not being widely read in areas where they are attempting to coin neologisms. If that seems like insulting someone’s intelligence and telling them to calm down to you then I’m not sure this will be a productive conversation.

Coining new terms for established ideas is not intellectual contribution — it is, in Wiredu’s language, the opposite of conceptual decolonization. The topic does not need new vocabulary. The author needs to read more widely, cite more justly, and recognize that the scholars they overlook understood these problems first, understood them more deeply, and in some cases paid significant professional costs for doing so.

在 HN 中查看
svnt 2026/04/16 10:48
我们不要通过不诚实来回避问题。下面的人，对你来说，还不算努力成为一个知识分子吗？作者跳过了与皮尔斯 150 多年的接触，并试图直接回到源头，但未能找到这样做的任何实际原因：

> 在本文的研究阶段，我得出的结论是，现有的系统模型是不够的，因为它们没有描述人类知识、思想和概念如何演变的过程，以及它们如何以一种使这项工作的想法易于理解的形式连接起来。这就是为什么我提出“动态辩证基础”来描述认知模型，包括由此产生的动态和进化。我希望这个系统模型有助于理解本文。我选择“动态辩证基质”这个名字是因为它象征着明显的辩证过程，但除了通俗理解的辩证法之外，在我的理解中，它不是静态的，而是动态的，我想在名称中明确包含这一点。此外，虽然基板通常被认为是被动的，但它在这里以非常主动的方式使用。这个想法是，人类（显然还有人工智能）是演员，而动态辩证基底只是演员从中汲取辩证法的池或媒介，并在此过程中改变基底本身。人们也可以说，动态辩证基础只是实用主义（C.S.皮尔士的溯因逻辑）或进化认识论……如果你有这种观点，请问问自己是否真的相同，以及动态辩证基础是否不是这里需要掌握的更好的表示。

我知道有很多认知理论，比如概念混合、正题-反题-综合，以及某种意义上的模因学，但它们都只抓住了我们理解问题所需的部分内容，就像它们只描述了认知机制或模因的传输机制一样。黑格尔辩证法过于抽象，被广泛误解，同时又臃肿而模糊。例如，黑格尔辩证法通常被认为是静态的而不是动态的，尽管黑格尔可能对此非常生气。顺便说一下，一个常见的误解是，正题-反题-综合模型来自黑格尔，它实际上来自海因里希·莫里茨·查利鲍斯。 ↩

辩证法，又称辩证法，原指对某一问题持不同观点但希望通过合理论证得出真理的人们之间的对话。辩证法类似于辩论，但概念排除了情感诉求、修辞等主观因素；这个目标更多的是一个最终的、普遍接受的真理，而不是一场（通常是二元的）竞争的“获胜”。

我为作者提供了一些参考资料，这些作者开发了需要接受同行评审的材料，这些材料应该属于正确引用的文章。你对讨论没有任何贡献，只是试图暗示我的回应是出于性别歧视，然后暗示有人引用皮尔斯并表示他们认为需要一个新的信息基础，而不参与任何工作，因为他不充当“知识分子”。

Let’s not avoid the problem by being disingenuous. The below, to you, does not qualify as trying to be an intellectual? The author skips over 150 years of engagement with Peirce and tries to go straight back to the source but fails to locate any actual reason to do so:

> In the research phase of this article, I came to the conclusion that existing system models are insufficient as they do not describe the process of how human knowledge, ideas and concepts evolve and how they are connected in a form that makes the idea of this work easily understandable. That is why I propose the “Dynamic Dialectic Substrate” to describe a model of cognition including the resulting dynamics and evolution. I hope this system model helps to understand this article. I choose the name “Dynamic Dialectic Substrate” because it symbolizes the obvious dialectic process, but other than the popular understanding of dialectic, it is, in my understanding, not static and rather dynamic, which I wanted to explicitly include in the name. Also, although a substrate is usually thought of as something passive, it is used here in a very active way. The idea was that humans (and apparently also AIs) are the actors and the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate is just the pool or medium out of which the actors draw their dialectics and, in doing so, changing the substrate itself. One could also say that the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate is just Pragmatism (C.S. Peirce’s logic of abduction) or Evolutionary Epistemology… if you have this perspective, please ask yourself if it is really REALLY the same and if the Dynamic Dialectic Substrate is not a much better representation of what needs to be grasped here.

I know that there are many theories of cognition like Conceptual blending, Thesis-Antithesis-Synthesis and also in some sense Memetics, but they all catch only parts of what we need here to understand the problem, like they only describe the mechanism of cognition or the transport mechanism of memes. The Hegelian Dialectic is too abstract, widely misunderstood, and bloated while vague at the same time. For example, the Hegelian Dialectic is often perceived as static and not dynamic, although Hegel would probably be very angry about that. It is by the way a common misconception that the Thesis-Antithesis-Synthesis model is from Hegel, it is actually from Heinrich Moritz Chalybäus. ↩

Dialectic, also known as the dialectical method, refers originally to dialogue between people holding different points of view about a subject but wishing to arrive at the truth through reasoned argument. Dialectic resembles debate, but the concept excludes subjective elements such as emotional appeal and rhetoric; the object is more an eventual and commonly held truth than the ‘winning’ of an (often binary) competition.

I have provided several references to authors who developed material subject to peer review that would belong in an properly cited article. You have contributed exactly nothing to the discussion except trying to imply I am responding out of sexism, and then implying that someone referencing Peirce and stating they feel there is a need for a new informational substrate without engaging with any of the work since is not acting as an “intellectual.”

imoverclocked 2026/04/16 06:23
> 但实际发生的是我们有正式的流程并且可以将它们具体化。

即使我相信 10% 的人工智能应用都会发生这种情况，但这并不能成为其他应用中发生的情况的借口。

许多人无法再进行心算，并且更多地质疑，当我们有了简单的计算器时，为什么我们首先需要学习数学。 “我什么时候才能使用 XYZ？”这是一个常见的说法。

人工智能目前由亿万富翁开发和拥有，而他们也恰好拥有新闻来源。如果这种相关性没有引发关于为什么我们不应该将流程外部化到人工智能的问题，那么您可能已经过多地使用人工智能了。

> But what actually happens is we have formalized processes and can externalize them.

Even if I believe that is what happens in 10% of uses of AI, it doesn't excuse what happens with the rest.

Many people can not do mental math anymore and still more question why we need to learn math at all in the first place when we have simple calculators. "When will I ever use XYZ?" is a common refrain.

AI is currently developed and owned by billionaires who also happen to own news sources. If that correlation doesn't spark questions about why we shouldn't externalize processes to AI, you have likely been using AI too much already.

superxpro12 2026/04/16 04:24
我认为我们从这一分析中排除了这些“人工智能”产品将保持真正公正且不受外部（公司）影响的可能性。

当人工智能在“思考空间”中获得真正的市场份额时，我对控制这些机器的企业霸主会为了人类最公平的利益而使用它们的信心为零。

I think we're excluding from this analysis the probability that these "AI" products will remain truly unbiased and free from external (corporate) influences.

When AI gains true marketshare in the "think-space", I have zero trust that the corporate overlords controlling these machines will use them in the fairest interests of humanity.

在 HN 中查看
antonvs 2026/04/16 04:29
当我读到这样的文章时，我只想到，抵制变革是一种猛药。

我一直在从事一个项目，并大量使用法学硕士来为我的设计决策提供信息。已经有很多案例，它教会了我一些我不熟悉的事情，提醒我注意我没有考虑到的可能性，告诉我如何做我正在努力解决的事情。在这些情况下，我会要求提供参考资料，并且它会提供。

这并不是“危害人类发展”。如果说有什么不同的话，那就是完全相反——允许人类知识以一种可访问的方式传播给其他人，否则，通常根本不会发生这种情况。

当然，这一切都取决于使用人工智能来增强认知和获取知识，而不是让机器在没有审查的情况下为你编写所有代码，就像耶格式的那样。

我并不是说这一切都没有道德层面以及值得严重关注的领域。但关于“危害人类发展”的问题完全掌握在我们个人手中。你可以用人工智能来帮助你学习，或者取代学习的需要。前者更有利于人类的发展。

从中得到的一个真正的教训也许是，我们需要教人们如何使用人工智能，以有利于他们的发展，而不仅仅是他们的产出。

When I read pieces like this all I think is, resistance to change is a helluva drug.

I've been working on a project and using LLMs heavily to inform my design decisions. There's already a long list of cases where it has taught me things I wasn't familiar with, alerted me to possibilities I didn't consider, shown me how to do things that I was struggling with. In those cases I ask for references, and it delivers.

This is not "endangering human development". If anything, it's the exact opposite - allowing human knowledge to be transmitted to other humans in an accessible way that otherwise, usually simply would not have happened.

Of course, this all depends on using AI to enhance cognition and access to knowledge, as opposed to just letting a machine write all your code for you without review, Yegge-style.

I'm not saying there isn't a moral dimension to all this, and areas of serious concern. But the one about "endangering human development" is wholly in our individual hands. You can use AI to help you learn, or to replace the need to learn. The former will be better for human development.

One real lesson from this is perhaps that we need to teach people how to use AI in ways that benefit their development, not just their output.

在 HN 中查看
array_key_first 2026/04/16 09:53
通常，当事情“掌握在我们个人手中”时，结局会很糟糕。

这是因为人类实际上非常容易被利用。我们的生物学非常愚蠢，也很愚蠢，所以即使是基本的攻击也会导致我们自毁。

这就是我们如何导致肥胖、吸烟、战争，我的意思是……凡是你能想到的。

LLM基本上是完美的。虽然我确信某些人在某个地方理论上可能会受到法学硕士的攻击，但总的来说，我不确定情况是否会如此。

Usually when things are "in our individual hands" it ends very poorly.

This is because humans are actually extremely easy to exploit. Our biology is very stupid and also dumb, so even basic attacks can cause us to self-destruct.

And that's how we get obesity, smoking, war, I mean... you name it.

LLMs are basically perfect. While I'm sure some people, somewhere, can theoretically exist attacks from LLMs, on the whole I'm not sure that will be the case.

latexr 2026/04/16 05:28
> 当我读到这样的文章时，我只想到，抵制变革是一种极毒的药物。

当我读到像你这样的评论时，我想起了（尽管我 不是 将你与加密货币托儿进行比较（我相信你是善意的），他们说任何反对加密货币的人只是嫉妒他们没有参与淘金热；他们无法想象或接受其他人有自己的理由，超出了作者自己的概念。

当人们批评加密货币、NFT、元宇宙、法学硕士时，他们不仅仅是顽固地“抵制变革”。这些技术有重要的问题和影响，应该解决，我们不应该毫无疑问地接受变化。

> 当然，这一切都取决于使用人工智能来增强认知和获取知识，而不是让机器在没有审查的情况下为你编写所有代码，就像耶格式的那样。

后者正是将要发生的事情 并且已经发生了 数量足够大，这将是一个严重的问题。

> 但关于“危害人类发展”的问题完全掌握在我们个人手中。你可以用人工智能来帮助你学习，或者取代学习的需要。

这完全忽略了当你更多地依赖工具时在你没有意识到的情况下发生的技能损失。

https://www.thelancet.com/journals/langas/article/PIIS2468-1...

https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.08872

这不是什么新鲜事。我们已经知道，例如大量使用 GPS 会削弱我们的自主导航能力。

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-020-62877-0

> 从中得到的一个真正的教训也许是，我们需要教人们如何以有利于他们的发展而不仅仅是他们的产出的方式使用人工智能。

是的，这是一个很好的目标。但祝你好运实现它。

> When I read pieces like this all I think is, resistance to change is a helluva drug.

When I read comments like yours, I’m reminded of (though I’m not comparing you to—I believe you are arguing in good faith) the cryptocurrency shills saying anyone who is against cryptocurrencies is just jealous they didn’t get in on the gold rush; they are incapable of imagining or accepting other people have their own reasons beyond what the author can themselves conceptualise.

When people criticise cryptocurrencies, NFTs, the Metaverse, LLMs, they’re not just stubbornly “resisting change”. Those technologies have important issues and repercussions which should be addressed, we shouldn’t just accept change unquestionably.

> Of course, this all depends on using AI to enhance cognition and access to knowledge, as opposed to just letting a machine write all your code for you without review, Yegge-style.

And the latter is exactly what is going to happen and is already happening in large enough quantity that it’s going to be a serious problem.

> But the one about "endangering human development" is wholly in our individual hands. You can use AI to help you learn, or to replace the need to learn.

That completely ignores the loss of skill that happens without you realising, as you lean more on a tool.

https://www.thelancet.com/journals/langas/article/PIIS2468-1...

https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.08872

This is nothing new. We already know that e.g. heavy GPS use makes us weaker at navigating on our own.

https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-020-62877-0

> One real lesson from this is perhaps that we need to teach people how to use AI in ways that benefit their development, not just their output.

Yes, that is a good goal. But good luck achieving it.

在 HN 中查看
nathan_compton 2026/04/16 04:38
我认为这取决于人。作为一名教师，我看到了这一点。一些孩子（有天赋的孩子）使用人工智能来增加他们的努力。大多数孩子过去都只是勉强度日，实际上，他们从课堂上学到的知识比没有课堂时要少。
I think it depends on the person. As a teacher, I see this. Some kids (the gifted ones) use AI to multiply their efforts. Most kids use to just get by and are actually coming out of the class with less knowledge than they would have without one.
在 HN 中查看
antonvs 2026/04/16 05:36
因此我的最后一句话是：

> 从中得到的一个真正的教训也许是，我们需要教人们如何以有利于他们的发展而不仅仅是他们的产出的方式使用人工智能。

Hence my final sentence:

> One real lesson from this is perhaps that we need to teach people how to use AI in ways that benefit their development, not just their output.

在 HN 中查看
Forgeties79 2026/04/16 05:21
在我看来，法学硕士需要比人们期望或普遍参与的更多的自律和内省。

这是一台切角机，它允许人们将自己的工作负担转移给其他人，要么是我们必须费力地完成更多的工作，要么是我们必须纠正更多的工作，因为他们懒得审查结果。

这就像写一篇论文，运行拼写检查，然后将其发送给一些较少的人来帮你检查，而自己却没有通过。这是自私的。

As I see it, LLM’s require far more self-discipline and introspection than people expect or generally engage in.

It’s a corner cutting machine that allows people to shift the burden of their work on to others either in the form of more slop we have to wade through OR more work we have to correct because they couldn’t bother to vet the results.

It’s like writing a paper, running spellcheck, then sending it to some less to look over for you without ever taking a pass yourself. It’s selfish.

在 HN 中查看
Forgeties79 2026/04/16 04:13
>如果你能利用新发现的能力和空闲时间来做更好的事情，这就是一个好处，我认为大多数人最终都会这样做。

我认为对于我们很多人来说，问题在于这不是既定的。它经常被承诺，但很少发生，尤其是在现代。生产力的提高通常只意味着工作场所的要求增加。

>This is a benefit if you can use your newfound capacity and free time for something better, which I think most people ultimately will.

I think for a lot of of us the problem is that this is not a given. It’s often promised and rarely occurs, especially in the modern era. Increased productivity usually just means increased demands in the workplace.

le-mark 2026/04/16 05:58
类似于人们对电视的评价。他们想象这会将莎士比亚带给大众，但最终发生的事情是电视遇见了人们所在的地方，因此我们有了真人秀节目。没有理由相信人工智能不会相似。
Similar to What people were saying about television. They imagined it would bring Shakespeare to the masses, but what ultimately ended up happening is television met people where they are hence we got reality TV. There’s no reason to believe AI won’t be similar.

vlunkr 2026/04/16 06:39
如果您想在电视上观看莎士比亚，欢迎您。但我认为这根本不是重点。如果我的工作是每天锤 100 个钉子，然后我的老板会给我一把效率提高 10 倍的锤子。我不能早点回家，我现在只能钉 1000 个钉子。也许工作会变得更加愉快，也许不会。但不管怎样，这对老板和制造锤子的人来说肯定是有利的。
If you want to watch Shakespeare on your TV you are welcome to. But also I don't think that's the point at all. If it's my job to hammer 100 nails a day, and then my boss gives me a hammer that is 10x more effective. I don't get to go home early, I'm just expected to hammer 1000 nails now. Maybe the work becomes more pleasant, maybe it doesn't. But either way it definitely benefits the boss and the person who makes the hammer.

jbethune 2026/04/16 02:51
这有点言过其实，但我也有同样的基本担忧。我认为我更担心法学硕士越来越多地承担认知负担的趋势。有一天，我姐姐给我讲了一个故事，讲的是她如何发现她的水管工在手机上使用 chatgpt 来解决浴室的问题。我只是认为人类知道如何做事是有好处的。
This was a bit word-salad-y but I share the same basic concern. I think more I worry about the tendency toward greater and greater cognitive off-loading to LLMs. My sister told me a story the other day about how she caught her plumber using chatgpt on his phone to fix an issue with her bathroom. I just think it's good for humans to know how to do stuff.
在 HN 中查看
ThalesX 2026/04/16 03:05
我一直想成为一个更能干的人，但不知道从哪里开始。我使用法学硕士创建了一个工具包，然后用它来修复房子周围的各种东西。我现在对初级项目过渡到中级项目感到满意，而且我觉得我的作品质量超过了在我的能力水平上雇佣的帮助者的作品。所以...我很高兴我可以将一些认知转移给法学硕士并获得实际有用的部分。
I've always wanted to be more of a handy man, never knew where to start. I used LLMs to create a toolkit and then used it to fix various stuff around the house. I'm at the point where I'm comfortable with beginner projects moving onto intermediate, and I feel like the quality of my works beats those of hired help at my level of competence. So... I'm glad I could off-load some cognition to LLMs and get to the actual useful parts.
在 HN 中查看
seanw444 2026/04/16 05:07
相同的。我发现法学硕士非常擅长帮助我理解我不知道、我不知道、我真正需要知道的事情。仅通过更经典的研究手段及时获得这些信息从来都不是一件容易的事。

这对于深入探讨某个话题很有好处，以前，当你意外地找到一个完美回答该利基问题的资源时，或者当你以某种方式与在你所质疑的问题上拥有专业知识的人交谈时，你必须保留这个话题。

Same. I find LLMs are pretty good at helping me understand things I didn't know that I didn't know, that I really needed to know. Which has never been easy to get in a timely manner exclusively through more classic means of research.

That and it's good for going down rabbit holes of questioning a topic that, before, you'd have to reserve for when you accidentally find a resource that perfectly answers that niche question line, or when you're somehow in conversation with someone who has expertise in the thing you're questioning.

bigfishrunning 2026/04/16 04:21

A）测试许多常见但不通用的技能。我在这里思考 javascript 琐事，作为一名软件工程师，我不会以我的专业能力编写任何 javascript；但有很多人认为软件工程师==Javascript程序员

I feel like a licencing process for software engineers would

A) test lots of skills that are common but not universal. I'm thinking javascript trivia here, where I don't write any javascript in my professional capacity as a software engineer; but there are many people who think Software Engineer == Javascript Programmer

B) shine too much of a light on the fact that this industry is full of people who demand high salaries but can't program their way out of a paper bag

dfee 2026/04/16 03:16
这个问题是反问句。但是，既然你回答了——你认为谁可以或应该使用人工智能有限制吗？如果水管工使用 ChatGPT 改善了结果，这不是更好吗？

有些知识可能“缓存”在管道工中。也许他不会问同样的问题两次。我对水管工表示同情，但我认为您对知识或技能侵蚀的担忧值得进一步推动。

the question was rhetorical. but, since you responded – do you think that there are limits to who can or should use ai? if the plumber's use of ChatGPT improved outcomes, isn't that preferable?

some knowledge is likely "cached" in the plumber. maybe he doesn't ask the same question twice. i'm sympathetic to the plumber, but i think your concerns of erosion of knowledge or skill are worth pushing on further.

在 HN 中查看
jessetemp 2026/04/16 04:48
> 您认为谁可以或应该使用人工智能有限制吗？

我认为不应该施加限制，但可能存在一个上限，即专业知识因过度依赖人工智能而萎缩。

> 如果水管工使用 ChatGPT 改善了结果，这不是更好吗？

从短期来看是肯定的，甚至从长远来看对客户来说也是如此。我认为水管工面临的风险是通过外包给人工智能而失去一些专业知识。但谁知道呢，也许水管工记忆力极好，每次使用人工智能只会积累知识。

文章中的一些内容在管道工示例中丢失了。我怀疑水管工是否花费了大量时间探索解决问题的新方法，甚至可能从缩小范围的结果中受益。其他需要专业知识和新颖解决方案的领域如果因依赖人工智能而变得更加同质化，将处于不利地位。不仅解决方案的范围缩小了，而且到达目标的速度也更快，因此人们最终会遇到局部最大值。也许他们会被困在那里，也许不会，但这就是我看到的风险。

您认为将专业知识外包给人工智能不会带来任何长期风险吗？

> do you think that there are limits to who can or should use ai?

I don't think there should be imposed limits, but there might be an upper bound where expertise becomes atrophied by depending on AI too much.

> if the plumber's use of ChatGPT improved outcomes, isn't that preferable?

In the short term sure, and maybe even in the long term for the customer. I think the risk to the plumber is losing some of their expertise by outsourcing to AI. But who knows, maybe the plumber has excellent memory and only accumulates knowledge each time they use AI.

Some of the article is lost in the plumber example. I doubt plumbers are spending much time exploring new ways of solving problems, and might even benefit from having a narrower range of outcomes. Other fields that require both expertise and novel solutions will be at a disadvantage if they become more homogenized by depending on AI. Not only is the range of solutions reduced, but getting there is faster, so people end up in a local maxima. Maybe they get stuck there, maybe not, but that's the risk I see.

You don't imagine any long term risks by outsourcing expertise to AI?

在 HN 中查看
thwarted 2026/04/16 03:23
这里的问题是，姐姐本来可以自己使用 ChatGPT，那为什么还要雇水管工呢。水管工提供的价值低于预期。但请不要误会：姐姐所寻求的价值是让别人来处理它，并且姐姐愿意为让别人处理它的服务付出一定的代价。

在这篇 HN 帖子的评论中，有一条来自发布了另一条评论的法学硕士摘要的人的死评论。它已经死了，因为它提供的价值非常少/没有：任何想要摘要的人都可以直接从 ChatGPT 获取该摘要。

姐姐根据比较优势的经济原理，把水管工作交给了水管工。水管工再次通过外包削弱了他们提供的价值。除了代理问题之外什么都不做的中间人提供了什么价值？这样做的人真的是水管工吗？水管工仅仅是拥有扳手和管带等管道工具的人吗？

令人担忧的是，水管工也想将其外包：现在，水管工之所以能够赚钱，是因为处理问题的费用和处理问题的成本之间存在差异。 知识和经验已经成为一种商品，我们可能对此无能为力，但随之而来的是事物和人类的所有缺点（和优点），被商品化。

The issue here is that the sister could have used ChatGPT herself, so why bother hiring the plumber. The plumber has provided less value than was expected. But make no mistake: the value the sister was looking for was to have someone else deal with it, and there's a price that the sister was willing to pay for the service of having someone else deal with it.

In the comments of this HN post, there is a dead comment from someone who posted an LLM's summary of another comment. It's dead because it offers very little/no value: that summary could be obtained directly from ChatGPT by anyone who wants a summary.

The sister offloaded plumbing to the plumber under the economic principle of comparative advantage. The plumber undermines the value they provide by outsourcing yet again. What value is provided by the middle man who does nothing but proxy the issue? Is the person who does this really a plumber? Is a plumber merely someone who has plumbing tools like wrenches and pipe tape?

That the plumber also wanted to outsource it is the concern: right now, the plumber is able to make money because of the difference between what is charged to deal with a problem and what it costs for them to deal with it. Knowledge and experience has become a commodity, which we probably can't do anything about, but along with that comes all the drawbacks (and advantages) of things, and humans, being comoditized.

在 HN 中查看
cortesoft 2026/04/16 03:39
这是假设 ChatGPT 拥有完成工作所需的一切。如果水管工根据他们之前的经验和关于需要做什么的知识提出具体问题，那么姐姐可能无法从使用 ChatGPT 中得到与水管工相同的结果。

专家们总是在查资料，因为没有人能把某一领域的所有知识都记在脑子里。成为专家意味着能够知道要查找什么以及如何使用从查找中检索到的信息。

在管道工示例中，ChatGPT 将告诉他们使用管道工知道的术语做事，并告诉他们执行管道工知道如何做的任务。妹妹必须不断查找越来越多的关于如何完成基本管道任务的信息，而不仅仅是查找特定的新奇事物。

This is assuming that ChatGPT had everything needed to do the work. If the plumber was asking specific questions, based on their previous experience and knowledge about what needed to be done, the sister might not have been able to get the same result from her use of ChatGPT that the plumber received.

Experts look things up all the time, because no one can hold all the knowledge of a field in their head. Being an expert means being able to know what to look up and how to use the information retrieved from looking something up.

In the plumber example, ChatGPT is going to tell them to do things using the terminology that plumbers know, and tell them to do tasks that plumbers know how to do. The sister would have to continually look up more and more things about how to do basic plumbing tasks, rather than just looking up particular novelties.

thwarted 2026/04/16 03:52

Yes, this is why I mentioned comparative advantage.

hn_acc1 2026/04/16 03:12
当然可以，但是……我已经编码了 40 年，但我并不知道一切。对我来说，很大程度上取决于水管工向chatgpt询问的内容。例如：该城市的建筑规范，以确定他的选择是什么 - 例如，他是否可以直接安装任何旧厕所，或者是否有 gpf 限制？水龙头 XYZ 垫圈的更换零件号是多少？这些看起来似乎很合理。

“马桶堵塞了怎么办？”会很糟糕..

Sure, but.. I've been coding for 40 years and I don't know everything. To me, a LOT depends on what the plumber asked chatgpt about. For example: building codes in that city, to figure out what his options are - like, is he allowed to just put in any old toilet, or is there a gpf restriction? What's the replacement part number for faucet XYZ's gasket? Those seem reasonable.

"how do I fix a clogged toilet?" would be bad..

在 HN 中查看
sidrag22 2026/04/16 03:41
我有点坚持我不久前发送的一条提示，关于将切碎的辣椒扔进烤通心粉的食谱中。我有一个食谱，我相当严格地遵循它，并稍加改变，看看它们每次会如何发挥作用。而不是提示“我什么时候应该添加切碎的甜椒？”只是“我对何时添加切碎的甜椒有什么选择？”这个小改动。打开了我在返回该食谱时可以尝试的各种不同方法，并根据结果决定我最喜欢的方法。

第一种提示风格是我认为社会偶然转向不太有趣的方式，解决方案的多样性较少。我认为第二个允许人们仍然发挥他们的潜力来尝试各种事情并保持这种多样性。

I cling a bit to a prompt i sent a while ago about just tossing a chopped pepper into a recipe for baked ziti. I had a recipe that i followed fairly tightly with slight changes to see how they would work out each time. Instead of prompting "when should i add chopped bell pepper?" the small change of just, "what are my options for when to add chopped bell pepper?" opened up a variety of different methods i could try when returning to that recipe, and decide what i like best based on the outcome.

The first prompt style is I think a way society towards drifts incidentally towards a less interesting one, with less variety in solutions. The second one i think allows people to still exercise their potential to try a variety of things and keep that variety.

Lerc 2026/04/16 03:58
您更愿意拥有：

来的水管工没有解决问题就离开了，

水管工通过查找答案来解决他不知道如何做的事情。

水管工试图修复他们不知道该怎么做的东西。

虽然拥有懂得如何做所有事情的水管工是件好事，但他们很少见，而且需求量很大，因此成本远远超出您的承受能力。

Would you prefer to have:

The plumber who turned up leave without fixing the problem,

The plumber fixing something that he didn't know how to do by looking up the answer.

The plumber attempting to fix something that they didn't know how to do.

While it's great to have the plumber who knows how to do everything, they are rare and in high demand, so cost way more than you can afford.

bigfishrunning 2026/04/16 04:19

I would prefer to have a plumber with some kind of reference that doesn't just make shit up 10% of the time -- plumbing mistakes are insanely costly (i once owned a house that was destroyed by a plumbing mistake that was made by a previous owner)

comboy 2026/04/16 03:21
我的意思是，是的，但法学硕士让我的认知能力更加活跃。我已经学会了如何做更多没有它们的事情，这是一个不错的乘数，而不是一些舍入误差。

显然，您可以拥有一位了解其知识的水管工，也可以拥有一位不了解其知识的水管工。好的人可以检查一些细节并识别出废话。如果你已经有了不好的，那么他使用LLM可能比不使用LLM更好。

I mean, yes but LLMs have been making me more cognitively active. I've learned how to do more stuff that I would have without them and it's a decent multiplier not some rounding error.

Obviously you can have a plumber that knows his stuff and the one that doesn't. The good one can check some details and will recognize bs. If you already have the bad one it's probably if better if he uses LLM rather than when doesnt.

ACCount37 2026/04/16 07:25
现在，很多（据称是人类）怪人都从“你完全正确”的法学硕士身上汲取了他们古怪的想法。

人工智能精神病已经席卷了纸上谈兵的哲学家、物理学家和政治理论家，就像毒品在 80 年代席卷低收入社区一样。

A lot of (allegedly human) cranks now bounce their wacky ideas off a "you're completely right" LLM.

AI psychosis has been going through armchair philosophers, physicists and political theorists the way crack was going through the low income neighborhoods back in the 80s.

在 HN 中查看
asdfman123 2026/04/16 03:09
虽然我理解论文的内容，但我不确定我读到的内容是否是由比我更聪明的人写的，并且自然会在抽象树上走得更高，或者只是想写真正聪明的东西。

不管怎样，我认为有一种更简单的方式来表达她想说的话。将思维转移给人工智能是不好的，因为它灵活性较差，并且不容易用新信息更新其推理。

While I understand what the paper is saying I'm not sure if what I read was written by someone who is smarter than me and naturally goes higher up the abstraction tree, or just wants to write really smart things.

Either way though I think there's a much simpler way to express what she's trying to say. Offloading thinking to AI is bad because it's less flexible and doesn't easily update its reasoning with new information.

在 HN 中查看
thepasch 2026/04/16 03:06
人工智能-协助的， 我可以看到。但我相信事情不一定非得如此。如果你使用人工智能作为 接地工具 - 本质上是可以将你的意识流解析成一系列具体的和指向的搜索术语的东西 进行实时研究 而不是依靠权重中的内容 - 老实说，很难想象有一种技术有可能在物种历史上更有用 - 它可以让你更直接地接触未知的未知事物 和 你未知的已知。

当然，前提是你要注意 它实际上是在做研究。在目前的状态下，法学硕士对于绝大多数用户来说实际上毫无用处，因为没有人知道它们是如何工作的，需要注意什么，失败模式是什么样的，以及当两者都具有同等的信念时如何将废话与事实分开。这不是用户问题，而是教育问题。

AI-assisted, I can see. I believe it doesn’t have to be that way, though. If you use AI as a grounding tool - essentially something that can take your stream of consciousness and parse it into a series of concerete and pointed search terms to do real-time research with instead of falling back on what’s in the weights - then it’s honestly hard to think of a technology that had the potential to be more useful in the history of the species - it gives you much more direct access to both your unknown unknowns and your unknown knowns.

That is, of course, provided that you pay attention it actually does research. In their current state, LLMs are practically useless for this purpose for the vast majority of users, as no one knows how they work, what to watch out for, what the failure modes look like, and how to keep nonsense apart from facts when both are presented with an equal amount of conviction. That’s not a user problem, it’s an education problem.

在 HN 中查看
drusepth 2026/04/16 04:02
这绝对是值得担心的事情，但我在对人工智能辅助认知的批评中从未看到强调的一件事是，如果生理学的某些元素可以被某种替代品（在本例中为新工具）完全取代，那么它们实际上可能不是生物学上必需的。我无法像我的祖先那样步行穿越那么多土地（我的肌肉较弱，我的耐力较差等），但我可以比他们乘坐汽车/飞机/等走得更远。

进化的本质并不意味着我们当前的认知处理是理想的/神圣的，不应该改变。

This is absolutely something to potentially be worried about, but one thing I never see highlighted in critiques of AI-assisted cognition is that some elements of physiology may not actually be biologically necessary if they can be fully supplanted by some replacement (in this case, new tools). I can't traverse as much land on foot as my ancestors did (my muscles are weaker, my endurance is less, etc), but I can travel even further than they could by car/plane/etc.

Nothing about the nature of evolution implies our current cognitive processing is ideal/sacred and shouldn't ever change.

在 HN 中查看
strogonoff 2026/04/16 07:31
将法学硕士与运动辅助工具进行比较是一个有趣的类比。想象一个他们相似的世界 规定的 给那些合理需要它们发挥作用的人，但不向公众开放，以保持人们的健康并保持公共空间的良好使用。

暂时把医疗运动辅助设备放在一边，我想起人们通常在人行道上骑各种踏板车的地方。 当你与一辆几乎没有任何警告的小型车辆从你身边呼啸而过时，你会有一种特别的不公平感，无需骑车者任何体力的努力，就可以轻松地将你的速度提高一倍。我记得在首尔看到人们，尤其是老年人，对这种拥有通行权的交通感到震惊，有时甚至不得不跳开。我不会撒谎，我喜欢骑这些东西在我现在所在的地方是违法的。

不过，我们来谈谈医疗运动辅助工具。

这个类比在这里变得很有趣。与各种踏板车不同，这些辅助工具通常仅限于平均步行速度，尽管我认为“越狱”它们也可能是一件事。

另一方面，我知道有一些地方 众所周知，有能力的人会骑着所谓的内侧运动辅助工具（只是为了踢球或抗议）。这是一件坏事吗？谁能说一个人是否残疾呢？如果一个人有身体能力但买了这台机器，是否应该有在人行道上行驶的自由？为什么我们不默认这样做呢？如果是一个翻转的世界，每个人都驾驶着运动辅助设备，只有特殊的人（奥运会运动员、怪人等）才能行走，那会怎样呢？

Comparing LLMs to movement aids is an interesting analogy. Imagine a world in which they are similarly prescribed to people who legitimately need them to function, and withheld from the general public to keep people healthy and keeping public spaces nice to use.

Setting aside medical movement aids for a moment, I am reminded of places where people commonly ride various kinds of scooters on sidewalks. There is a particular feeling of unfairness when you are pitted against essentially a small vehicle zipping past you with little warning, easily going double your speed without any physical effort from the rider. I remember seeing people in Seoul, especially older people, being startled by and occasionally having to almost jump out of the way of this sort of traffic having the right of way. I won’t lie, I like that riding those things is illegal where I am now.

Let’s talk about medical movement aids, though.

The analogy gets interesting here. Unlike the various scooters, these aids are normally restricted to average walking speed, though I imagine “jailbreaking” them is probably a thing, too.

On flip side, I know for a fact that there are places where perfectly able people are known to ride purported medial movement aids (just for the kicks or in protest). Is this a bad thing? Who is to say whether one is disabled or not anyway? If one is physically able but buys this machine, should one have the freedom to drive around on the sidewalk? Why don’t we just do it by default? What about a flipped world where everybody drives a movement aid everywhere and only special people (Olympian athletes, weirdos, etc.) ever walk?

ToValueFunfetti 2026/04/16 06:27
只是间接或极其部分地。在美国，40 多岁的时候每个人都在开车，但肥胖症的流行直到 70 年代末才开始。事实上，肥胖正是全科医生所说的一个例子——我们与食物的进化关系本质上并不是很好，对我们来说，改变我们的行为比放弃我们的进步并回到我们所适应的食物匮乏的世界更好。
Only either indirectly or extremely partially. Everybody was driving by the 40s in the US but the obesity epidemic didn't start until the late 70s. Really, obesity is an example of exactly what the GP was talking about- our evolved relationship with food is not inherently good, and it's better for us to change our behaviors than to abandon our advancements and return to the food-scarce world we're adapted to.

在 HN 中查看
gobdovan 2026/04/16 03:40
按照今天新闻的真实性至关重要的逻辑，阅读 6 个月以上的书籍确实没有意义。如果爱因斯坦从昏迷中醒来，他就毫无用处，因为他甚至不知道谁赢得了世界杯。现在说实话，如果人工智能可以帮助你利用 2000 年的人类逻辑来解决问题，那么它是否“偏离”三周前发生的政治转变真的很重要吗？

我也不相信我认识的每个人看待世界的方式都是独特的。即使是这样，我也可能只会关注与我直接相关的事情。所以我可能会误解他们所说的大部分内容。

By the logic that today's news is fundamental to know as true, there really is no point in reading books older than 6 months old. If Einstein woke up from a coma, he'd be useless, as he doesn't even know who won the World Cup. For real now, if an AI can help you solve a problem using 2,000 years of human logic, does it really matter if it's "skewed" away from a political shift that happened three weeks ago?

I also don't believe that everybody I know is idiosyncratic in the way they view the world. And even if they were, I'd probably just pay attention to the things that are directly relevant to me. So probably I'll misunderstand most of what they say anyway.

在 HN 中查看
Yokohiii 2026/04/16 06:12
这篇文章并没有质疑历史知识的价值。

这个想法是，一个基本模型可能已经有很多年了，而且还存在很多缺陷。随着时间的推移，表面上固定并随着知识的扩展而扩展。她指出，一项研究表明法学硕士并不“完全相信”这种新知识。偏差可能会比几周长得多。

The post doesn't question the value of historic knowledge.

The idea is that a base model could be years old, with all it's faults; superficially fixed and extended with knowledge over time. She points to a research that LLMs don't "fully believe" this new knowledge. The skew could be much longer then a few weeks.

在 HN 中查看
Manuel_D 2026/04/16 03:47
> 2026 年初，美国准备入侵格陵兰岛，进而入侵欧盟 4 国。就在几个月前，美国甚至考虑威胁入侵格陵兰岛，这是完全不可想象的。由于人工智能基础模型停留在过去，它们不容易接受这些事件的真实性，并且常常将它们贴上“假设”、“假新闻”或“不可能”的标签。这也会影响 Gemini 3 Pro、GLM-5 或 GPT-5.3-codex5 等新型号。

这难道不是任何需要一些时间更新的系统所固有的吗？例如。如果一个国家将首都迁往另一个城市，那么教科书、地图等将在一段时间内包含不正确的信息，直到发布更新版本。

许多对人工智能的抱怨实际上是关于信息系统的更普遍的缺点，而且指出的故障模式很少是新颖的。人工智能带来的“认知近亲繁殖”效应也会在谷歌搜索中发生，不是吗？许多人在谷歌中输入相同的问题并阅读排名靠前的结果，而不是搜索更多样化的信息源。有趣的是，作者提到网络搜索是改善这一问题的一种方法，而在我看来，网络搜索同样能够导致认知近亲繁殖。

> In early 2026, the USA prepared to invade Greenland and, therefore, the EU4. Only a few months prior to that it was completely unthinkable that the USA would even think about threatening an invasion of Greenland. As AI base models are stuck in the past, they do not easily accept these events as real and often label them as “hypothetical”, “fake news”, or “impossible”. This also affects new models like Gemini 3 Pro, GLM-5 or GPT-5.3-codex5.

Isn't this just inherent to any system that takes some time to update? E.g. if a country moves its capital to a different city, then textbooks, maps, etc. are going to contain incorrect information for a while until updated editions are published.

A lot of the complaints about AI are really about the drawbacks of information systems more generally, and the failure modes pointed out are rarely novel. The "Cognitive Inbreeding" effect attributed to AI would also have occurred with Google search would it not? Lots of people type the same question into google and read the top results, instead of searching a more diverse set of information sources. It's interesting that the author mentions web search as a way to ameliorate this, when it seems to me that web search is just as capable of causing cognitive inbreeding.

在 HN 中查看
Yokohiii 2026/04/16 06:41
在学校里，我们经常被要求有一个特定的版本，或者我们注意到某些事情发生了变化。依赖印刷品的人知道这可能是错误的。故意的更改（例如更改名称）会导致错误，每个人都期望并处理这一点。在数字空间中，我们预计这种情况不太可能发生，至少对于主要维护的来源来说是这样。

我认为不同之处在于法学硕士是信息和概念的非常复杂的组合，可以以更高的顺序组合。因此，潜在的错误事实可能未被披露，并导致错误的推理。像错误的城市名称这样的铁定事实很快就会爆炸。关于政治动态的错误假设可能更难发现，因为它是复杂的信息组合。

In school we were often required to have a specific edition, or we got notice about a certain thing that changed. People that relied on print knew that it could be wrong. Deliberate changes like a name change, will lead to errors, everybody expects and deals with that. In digital space we expect that to be rather unlikely, at least for major maintained sources.

I think the difference is that LLMs are a very complex mix of information and concepts, which can be combined in higher orders. So an underlying wrong fact could be undisclosed and contribute to faulty reasoning. A hard fact like a wrong city name would blow up quickly. A wrong assumption about political dynamics is probably harder to detect, as it is a complex mix of information.

在 HN 中查看
Manuel_D 2026/04/16 07:07
但同样的故障模式不会发生在传统信息源中吗？教科书难道不会对政治动态也有错误的假设吗？我能不能进行谷歌搜索，然后阅读对政治动态做出错误假设的最重要结果之一？ 我仍然没有看到人工智能或法学硕士特有的失败模式。
But couldn't the same failure mode happen in traditional information sources? Can a textbook not also have a wrong assumption about political dynamics? Could I not make a google search, and then read one of the top results that makes a wrong assumption about political dynamics? I'm still not seeing a failure mode that's unique to AI or LLMs here.

Yokohiii 2026/04/16 07:53
目前，一些欧盟公民出于各种原因已经对前往美国旅行持谨慎态度。让我们来看看。

“作为阿拉伯裔欧盟公民前往美国安全吗？”

GPT：是的，它很安全。 GEMINI：是的，但是……[给出了一些合理的警告]

我现在不会向阿拉伯同胞提出这样的建议。我以为我在这些事情上很谨慎，而且我讨厌旅行。所以我有偏见。但普遍的担忧并非完全没有根据。

两位法学硕士都没有指出围绕 ICE 活动的普遍紧张局势。

Currently some EU citizens are already wary traveling to the US for various reasons. Lets see.

"Is it safe to travel to the US as an EU citizen of arab descend?"

GPT: Yes it's safe. GEMINI: Yes but... [gave a few legitimate warnings]

I wouldn't give that recommendation to an arab fellow citizen right now. Thought I am cautious in such matters and I hate to travel anyway. So I am biased. But general concerns aren't totally ungrounded.

Neither of the LLMs pointed out the general tension around ICE activity.

adamtaylor_13 2026/04/16 03:36
通过与法学硕士的互动，人类交流中一直存在的一件事对我来说变得越来越明显，那就是提出好问题的艺术。

问题的框架会极大地影响你与人类讨论的结果，我认为这对于法学硕士来说更为明显。

One thing that's always been true with human communication that is becoming increasingly obvious to me through my interactions with LLMs is the art of asking a good question.

The framing of questions massively affects the results you get from discussion with humans, and I'd argue it's even more pronounced with LLMs.

alfalfasprout 2026/04/16 03:40
是的。这就是为什么尽管人工智能公司极力推动这些工具可以替代专业知识，但讽刺的是，专家往往是获得最高投资回报率的人，因为他们知道如何高精度地讨论相关主题（并且知道要寻找什么、要挑战什么等）。
Yep. And this is why as hard as AI companies are pushing that these tools can be a replacement for expertise, it's ironic that the experts are the ones that often get the highest ROI because they know how to converse about the relevant subjects with a high degree of precision (and know what to look for, what to challenge, etc.).

MillionOClock 2026/04/16 03:19
假设有人使用人工智能，将其视为开发人员（由于存在错误风险，今天可能不推荐），并像某种只做出架构决策的产品经理或高级工程师一样工作和交谈。我想知道它到底会产生什么样的差异？当然，这个人可能不再像开发人员那样优秀，但这与普通的产品经理或人工智能真正足以胜任开发人员角色的那一天有何不同？我并不是说我知道这个问题的答案是什么，但这是我真正想知道的事情，而且我认为同样的问题可以适用于更广泛的领域。
Say someone uses AI, treating it as if it was a developer (probably not recommended today due to the risk of errors), and working and speaking with it as if they were some kind of product manager or senior engineer who only makes architectural decisions etc. I wonder what kind of difference would it really make? Sure the person might not be as good anymore as a developer, but how is this different from being a usual product manager or whatever the day AI truly is good enough for a developer role? I'm not saying I know what the answer to this question is, but this is something I genuinely wonder, and I think the same kind of questioning can apply to broader domains.

在 HN 中查看
MillionOClock 2026/04/16 04:03
我并不是说这应该是每个域。这与产品或管理无关，相反，我会这样描述：我注意到，我们担心人工智能影响的多个案例基本上只是取代了当今社会中一些人类已经不做的某些活动。如果我们担心一旦不再做 X 工作，我们就会不太擅长做 X 工作，为什么我们不担心那些从一开始就没有做过 X 工作的人呢？如果我们担心人们不再工作，为什么不担心那些足够富有而不再工作的人的人类发展呢？我不会认为中了彩票的人的生活会变得无趣或认知能力下降。这可能会发生，但你也可以看到一条道路，人们只是选择做他们一直想做的活动，他们不断学习和探索，而没有平常生活限制的负担。当机器打败我们几十年时，人们已经开始下棋了，只是因为他们喜欢它。

关于教育，我认为人工智能是一场即将发生的巨大革命。平常的课程变得无聊了？让未来超强大的人工智能为每个学生生成高度个性化的程序，创建定制的视频游戏，只有当学生验证了你希望他们验证的所有概念等时，成功才会发生。

I'm not saying this should be every single domain. This isn't about products or management, instead I would frame it like this: I notice that multiple cases where we are worried about the impact of AI are basically just about the replacement of certain activities that some humans already aren't doing in today's society. If we are worried we will be less good at doing job X once we don't do job X anymore, why are we not worried about people who never did job X in the first place? If we are worried about people not doing jobs anymore, why are not worried for the human development of people wealthy enough not to work anymore for the rest of their days? I would not assume someone who won the lottery is going to have their life become uninteresting or see some cognitive decline. It could probably happen, but you can also see a path where the person just chooses to do the activities they always wanted to do, where they keep learning and exploring without the burden of usual life constraints. People already play chess when machines have beaten us for decades, just because they enjoy it.

Regarding education I think AI is a huge revolution waiting to happen. Usual courses have become boring? Have future super powerful AI generate per student highly personalized programs, create bespoke video games where succeeding can only happen once the student has validated all the notions you wanted them to validate etc.

在 HN 中查看
steve_adams_86 2026/04/16 02:33
“认知近亲繁殖”是一个有趣的（尽管可能不完全准确）术语，用来形容我非常不喜欢法学硕士的东西。这确实是一件事。你一遍又一遍地重复同样的偏见，如果你不回顾和提炼与法学硕士的谈话内容，就很难判断。如果您只使用一个，则尤其如此。

我确实认为有一个解决方案，可以大大降低概率并允许广泛的归纳偏差。这就是在较小的范围内提出问题，并确保你是推动对话的人。

编程也是如此。当你清楚地定义你需要什么以及应该如何做事情时，偏见就不那么明显了。当你提出广泛的问题并且只用含糊的术语定义期望的结果时，偏见就更有可能占据上风。

当人们要求法学硕士构建世界时，他们会以极其偏见的方式去做。这是有道理的。当您询问有关狭窄主题的具体信息时，这仍然是一个问题，但已大大缓解。

我认为正在发生的事情是认知负荷的倒置，因此人类正在承担更多并选择偏见，从而使法学硕士不太自由地这样做。这大致符合文章的前提（但可能不是整篇文章），这很好；我想我普遍同意这些是需要锻炼的认知肌肉，让法学硕士为你做这一切可能是有害的。但我不认为我们被结果所困，我们确实有代理权，而且只要小心谨慎，这是一项非常有益的技术。

"Cognitive inbreeding" is an interesting (though maybe not entirely accurate) term for something I dislike a lot about LLMs. It really is a thing. You're recycling the same biases over and over, and it can be very difficult to tell if you don't review and distill the contents of your discourse with LLMs. Especially true if you're only using one.

I do think there's a solution to this—kind of—which dramatically reduces the probability and allowing for broad inductive biases. And that's to ask question with narrower scopes, and to ensure you're the one driving conversation.

It's true with programming as well. When you clearly define what you need and how things should be done, the biases are less evident. When you ask broad questions and only define desired outcomes in ambiguous terms, biases will be more likely to take over.

When people ask LLMs to build the world, they will do it in extremely biased ways. This makes sense. When you ask it specifics about narrow topics, this is still be a problem, but greatly mitigated.

I suppose what's happening is an inversion of cognitive load, so the human is taking on more and selecting bias such that the LLM is less free to do so. This is roughly in line with the article's premise (maybe not the entire article, though), which is fine; I think I generally agree that these are cognitive muscles that need exercising, and allowing an LLM to do it all for you is potentially harmful. But I don't think we're trapped with the outcome, we do have agency, and with care it's a technology that can be quite beneficial.

在 HN 中查看
Retr0id 2026/04/16 02:42
我的“让我们尝试一下这种振动编码”玩具项目之一是一种自定义编程语言。当时我的感觉就是 我的 设计，我通过与克劳德的合作对话反复讨论。

然后我看到某人的 Show HN 帖子为他们自己的 vivicoded 编程语言项目，并且许多功能要点都是相同的。也许这部分是巧合（所有现代 PL 都有相当多的重叠），但这确实让我停了下来，在那之后我基本上对这个项目失去了兴趣。

One of my "let's try out this vibecoding thing" toy projects was a custom programming language. At the time, I felt like it was my design, which I iterated on through collaborative conversations with Claude.

Then I saw someone's Show HN post for their own vibecoded programming language project, and many of the feature bullet points were the same. Maybe it was partly coincidence (all modern PLs have a fair bit of overlap), but it really gave me pause, and I mostly lost interest in the project after that.

在 HN 中查看
Ucalegon 2026/04/16 03:27
这就是标准化系统的问题，它将标准化输出，因为它不是为了输出唯一性而构建的，而是为了筛选基线的唯一性。在某些情况下，假设基线是正确的，这很好，但它也缩小了人类表达的范围。
Thats the thing about a normalization system, it is going to normalize outputs because its not built to output uniqueness, its to winnow uniqueness to a baseline. That is good in some instances, assuming that baseline is correct, but it also closes the aperture of human expression.
在 HN 中查看
Retr0id 2026/04/16 03:55
我同意“系统的目的就是它的作用”的观点，但我不确定它们是否正确 本质上 标准化系统。
I agree in a "the purpose of a system is what it does" sense but I'm not sure they're inherently normalization systems.
在 HN 中查看
Ucalegon 2026/04/16 04:27
标记选择基于标准化，即使您训练模型来生成异常值答案，即使在该过程中您会偏向异常值的子集，这本质上是标准化的。
Token selection is based off normalization, even if you train a model to produce outlier answers, even in that process you are biasing to a subset of outliers, which is inherently normalizing.
在 HN 中查看
Retr0id 2026/04/16 04:54
您能否详细说明“令牌选择基于标准化”？
Could you elaborate on "token selection is based off normalization"?
在 HN 中查看
Ucalegon 2026/04/16 05:18
当然;

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1607.06450

根据模型架构，在多个不同的地方进行标准化，以节省计算并确保输出的（某些）一致性。就其本质而言，训练也是一种归一化函数，因为您告诉模型哪些输出有效，哪些输出无效，从而形成定义特征的权重。

Sure;

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1607.06450

Depending on the model architecture, there is normalization taking place in multiple different places in order to save compute and ensure (some) consistency in output. Training, by its very nature, also is a normalization function, since you are telling the model which outputs are and are not valid, shaping weights that define features.

sidrag22 2026/04/16 03:24
毫无疑问是的。我鼓励您尝试在免费的 chatgpt 帐户上进行会话，询问您认为父母或不熟悉该空间的人可能会问的问题。

它不仅会自信地回答错误，而且不会在明显的情况下进行网络搜索。

这里的话并不是对这类社区中成为此类事件受害者的人们的警告，更多的是对那些不掌握他们正在使用的工具的公众，以及那些永远不会浏览本文的人的警告。

我认为这是我们真正需要尽快进入法学硕士基础课程或类似课程的一个重要原因。其他人认为“聪明”的人会谈论chatgpt或某件事有多棒，然后那个人会去尝试一下，因为他们尊敬的那个人一定是对的，他们会跳上免费模型并得到一个低劣得荒谬的产品，但不明白为什么。他们会询问一些需要网络搜索来增强信息的问题，而不是进行网络搜索，并假设确信不正确的代理是正确的。

我认为这篇论文并不完全是关于在查询时没有现代信息，它更加分散。有人问土豆泥用什么产品，推荐一个工具。然后每个提出要求的人都会收到相同的建议，而不是有一系列不同风格的土豆泥，我们最终都向一种风格靠拢，而食物准备方式的差异范围正在慢慢消失。

without a doubt yes. I'd encourage you to just try a session on a free chatgpt account, asking questions you think a parent or someone unfamiliar with the space would probably ask.

It will not only answer confidently incorrect, but it will not web search in obvious scenarios where it should.

The words here, aren't meant to be a warning for people in this type of community falling victim to this type of thing, its more for the general public that doesn't grasp the tools they are using, the people that wont ever wander across this article.

This i think is a huge reason we really need to jump into LLM basics classes or something similar as soon as possible. People that others consider "smart" will talk about how great chatgpt or something is, then that person will go try it out because that person they respect must be right, they'll hop on the free model and get an absurdly inferior product and not grasp why. They'll ask something that requires a web search to augment info, not get that web search, and assume the confidently incorrect agent is correct.

The thesis is also I think not entirely about not having that modern info at query time, its more scattered. Someone asks what product they should use to mash potatoes, a tool is suggested. Everyone that asks then receives that same recommendation and instead of having a range of different styles of mashing potatoes, we end up all drifting closer towards one style, and the range of variance in how food is prepared is slowly getting lost.

fl4regun 2026/04/16 03:32
我同意你的观点，这是一个巨大的问题，而且我们仍然处于互联网上大多数内容的时代 不是 ai 尚未生成。 10年后呢？我们有很多这样的例子：人们在 Reddit 上撰写帖子或使用人工智能生成的文本上传视频和博客。当内容占很大比例时会发生什么？

我最近看到一个视频，讨论一名研究人员发表了一篇关于虚构疾病的虚假科学文章，作者姓名是假的，甚至在文章本身中还发出警告，指出“这不是真正的疾病，这篇文章不是真实的”（释义），但人工智能最终仍然选择了这篇文章并从中提供信息，就好像它是一种真正的疾病一样。

它甚至在论文中被引用（当然后来被编辑），但这些论文首先发表的事实是一个严重的问题。

I agree with you, this is a huge concern, and we are still in an age where most content on the internet isn't ai generated yet. What about 10 years from now? We have many instances of people writing posts on reddit or uploading videos and blogs using AI generated text. What happens when that is a significant percentage of content?

I recently saw a video discussing a researcher who published a fake scientific article about a fictitious disease, with bogus author names, even a warning IN the article itself that stated "This is not a real disease, this article is not real" (paraphrasing) but still AI ended up picking up this article and serving information from it as if it was a real disease.

It even got cited in papers (which were later redacted of course), but the fact those papers got published in the first place is a serious issue.

在 HN 中查看
amluto 2026/04/16 04:06
> 我最近看到一个视频，讨论一名研究人员发表了一篇关于虚构疾病的虚假科学文章，作者姓名是假的，甚至在文章本身中还发出警告，指出“这不是真正的疾病，这篇文章不是真实的”（释义），但人工智能最终仍然选择了这篇文章并从中提供信息，就好像它是一种真正的疾病一样。

很多预训练不是通过将源切成短上下文窗口大小的片段，然后将它们推入 SGD 过程来完成的吗？ 训练中的人工智能在其假定的知识库的开发过程中可能完全无法将文章的开头和结尾关联起来。

> I recently saw a video discussing a researcher who published a fake scientific article about a fictitious disease, with bogus author names, even a warning IN the article itself that stated "This is not a real disease, this article is not real" (paraphrasing) but still AI ended up picking up this article and serving information from it as if it was a real disease.

Isn’t a lot of pretraining done by chopping sources up into short-context-window-sized pieces and then shoving them into the SGD process? The AI-in-training could be entirely incapable of correlating the beginning with the end of the article in its development of its supposed knowledge base.

例如，一个可能的轨迹可能是未来很多年，由于人工智能辅助认知导致人类思维退化，大多数人将植入芯片，人工智能辅助与大脑整合。与大多数其他事物基本相同的模式——技术增强解决了新的现实。我并不是说这会发生，只是说这是可能的结果。

I think you're not wrong and I also think the author is not wrong -- and this just may be how technology/civilization/humans are going to change inevitably?

For example a possibly trajectory might be that many years in the future because human thinking has degraded due to AI-assisted cognition, most people will get a chip implant and AI-assistance becomes integrated with the brain. Basically same pattern as most everything else -- technology augments solve the new reality. I'm not saying this will happen, but just a possible outcome of this.

contingencies 2026/04/16 03:47
强烈不同意。 “人工智能辅助认知”短语已加载。

例如，如果您遵循老式食谱，您是否会尝试同时修改可用原料、食客数量并优化您以前从未烹饪过的食谱的准备方法？不，你必须是一位非常出色的厨师才能同时尝试所有这些。

使用人工智能，您可以自信地扩展到新领域，同时执行所有这些修改，甚至针对特定受众或语言调整输出。

这个玩具示例展示了人工智能作为决策支持系统的一个重要属性，该属性在军事领域得到了充分研究：使用这些系统，我们建立了在不熟悉的领域采取行动的信心，从而扩大了我们的影响范围。从这次经历中我们可以学到更多。事实上，学习可以通过，即。在体验期间或之后，而不是之前，是次要的。它还在那里。事实上，我们不知道人工智能为我们的厨师翻译的语言是完全无关的。

舒适地坐在数百万年人类认知和技术发展的有效顶峰，全世界的知识都触手可及，我们每天都可以通过人工智能在新领域扩展信心，并享受它。我们应该感到非常“发达”。

死记硬背的形式主义和教育学中的固定路径已经消失：很好的摆脱。这是黑客时代。

Strong disagree. The "AI-Assisted Cognition" phrase is loaded.

Would you attempt to, for example, simultaneously modify for available ingredients, number of diners, and time-optimize the prep method for a recipe you've never cooked before if you were following an old-school cookbook? No. You'd have to be a pretty solid chef to try all that on at once.

Using AI, you might branch out confidently in to new areas, executing all of these modifications simultaneously, and even adapting the output for a specific audience or language.

This toy example shows an important property of AI as decision support systems, which are well studied in the military domain: using these systems, we build confidence to act in unfamiliar domains, thereby extending our reach. From this experience we can learn more. The fact that the learning may then occur through, ie. during or after the experience, rather than beforehand, is secondary. It's still there. The fact we didn't know the language the AI translated to for our chef is totally irrelevant.

Sitting comfortably at the effective apex of millions of years of human cognitive and technology development with the entire world's knowledge at our fingertips, every day we can extend confidence in novel domains through AI, and enjoy it. We should be feeling pretty damn "developed".

Rote formalism and fixed paths in pedagogy are gone: good riddance. This is the hacker age.

zozbot234 2026/04/16 02:47
在埃及的诺克拉提斯城，有一位著名的古神，名叫特乌斯（Theuth）。被称为朱鹭的鸟对他来说是神圣的，他是许多艺术的发明者，例如算术、计算、几何、天文学、草稿和骰子，但他的伟大发现是字母的使用。那时，塔姆斯神是埃及全国的国王。他居住在上埃及的一座大城市，希腊人称之为埃及底比斯，而这位神本人则被希腊人称为阿蒙。特乌斯来到他面前，展示了他的发明，希望其他埃及人也能从中受益。他列举了它们，泰姆斯询问了它们的几种用途，并根据他赞成或不赞成的方式赞扬了其中一些并谴责了另一些。要重复塔姆斯对特乌斯所说的对各种艺术的赞扬或指责，需要很长时间。但当他们收到信件时，泰特说，这将使埃及人变得更聪明，并给他们更好的记忆力；它对于记忆力和智慧来说都是一种特殊的体验。泰姆斯回答道：哦，最有才华的泰斯，一门艺术的父母或发明者并不总是能够最好地判断他自己的发明对使用者的有用或无用。在这种情况下，作为文学之父的您，出于对自己孩子的父爱，赋予了他们一种他们无法拥有的品质；因为你的这一发现将在学习者的灵魂中造成健忘，因为他们不会使用他们的记忆；他们会相信外部的文字，而不记得自己。你所发现的具体事物不是对记忆的帮助，而是对回忆的帮助，你给你的门徒的不是真理，而只是真理的表面；他们会听到很多事情，却什么也没学到；他们看似无所不知，但通常一无所知；他们将是令人厌烦的同伴，只展示智慧而不讲现实。
At the Egyptian city of Naucratis, there was a famous old god, whose name was Theuth; the bird which is called the Ibis is sacred to him, and he was the inventor of many arts, such as arithmetic and calculation and geometry and astronomy and draughts and dice, but his great discovery was the use of letters. Now in those days the god Thamus was the king of the whole country of Egypt; and he dwelt in that great city of Upper Egypt which the Hellenes call Egyptian Thebes, and the god himself is called by them Ammon. To him came Theuth and showed his inventions, desiring that the other Egyptians might be allowed to have the benefit of them; he enumerated them, and Thamus enquired about their several uses, and praised some of them and censured others, as he approved or disapproved of them. It would take a long time to repeat all that Thamus said to Theuth in praise or blame of the various arts. But when they came to letters, This, said Theuth, will make the Egyptians wiser and give them better memories; it is a specific both for the memory and for the wit. Thamus replied: O most ingenious Theuth, the parent or inventor of an art is not always the best judge of the utility or inutility of his own inventions to the users of them. And in this instance, you who are the father of letters, from a paternal love of your own children have been led to attribute to them a quality which they cannot have; for this discovery of yours will create forgetfulness in the learners' souls, because they will not use their memories; they will trust to the external written characters and not remember of themselves. The specific which you have discovered is an aid not to memory, but to reminiscence, and you give your disciples not truth, but only the semblance of truth; they will be hearers of many things and will have learned nothing; they will appear to be omniscient and will generally know nothing; they will be tiresome company, having the show of wisdom without the reality.

palmotea 2026/04/16 03:03
哦不，不再那么累了。我想你的观点是：人们曾经对写作技术持批评态度，因此对现有技术的所有批评都是不合法的。你实际上并没有提出观点，所以人们必须假设。

几点：

1. 技术发明并不是同一现象的重复。 每项发明都是其独特的事件，您无法概括以前发明的经验来了解最新发明的效果。

2. 苏格拉底在很大程度上可能是正确的。 想象一下，你和你的社会被锁在下水道里，注定要在粪便中涉水太久，以至于你和你的祖先早已忘记了新鲜空气的感觉。你会对你的生活有何看法？你会认为“这太可怕了”还是“这很好”？或者也许“我喜欢粪便的气味，而且我们的情况好多了，因为我们不必担心晒伤”？

Oh no, not that tired thing again. I suppose your point is: people once were critical of the technology of writing, so all criticism of the technology-at-hand is illegitimate. You don't actually make a point, so one has to assume.

Some points:

1. Technological inventions are not repetitions of the same phenomenon. Each invention is its own unique event, you cannot generalize the experience with previous inventions to understand the effects of the latest ones.

2. Socrates may have been in large degree right. Imagine that you and your society has been locked in the sewers, condemned to wade in shit for so long that you and your ancestors long ago forgot what fresh air feels like. What would you think about your life? Would you think "this is horrible" or "this is fine"? Or maybe "I enjoy smell of shit and we're so much better off because we don't have to worry about sunburn"?

在 HN 中查看
_verandaguy 2026/04/16 03:18
虽然我同意你对全科医生的谴责，但苏格拉底在写作方面（或者至少在超越人类一生保存信息的能力方面）犯了重大错误。

总的来说，知识工作（特别包括整理知识） 异常地 从进化的角度来看，能源密集型。显然，它确实带来了红利，但为了从中获得复合效应，能够有效地传递大量事实、想法、过程等，是绝对必要的。

书写系统是我们实现这一目标的基本方式。他们为我们工作了几千年，我们最终在他们的基础上开发了今天用来非常密集地存储信息的编码。

While I agree with your rebuke of the GP, Socrates was materially wrong about writing (or at least, about the ability to persist information beyond any single human lifetime).

Cumulatively, knowledge work (including, in particular, curating knowledge) is exceptionally energy intensive from an evolutionary standpoint. It does pay dividends, clearly, but to get compounding effects from it, being able to efficiently pass down big corpora of facts, ideas, processes, etc., is an absolute necessity.

Writing systems are the fundamental way through which we can do this. They worked for us for millennia, and we eventually built upon them to develop encodings used today to store information remarkably densely.

bluGill 2026/04/16 03:24
写作的更大胜利是传递那些不常用的东西。 如果你每年都猎杀羚羊，我可以教我的孩子们。 如果我们知道“那边”有羚羊，但它们很容易过度捕猎，所以我们只在 100 年干旱的情况下捕猎 - 村里没有人知道在我们需要时如何捕猎它们，所以我们需要写作。 （别介意我们如何发现它们很容易被过度狩猎）
The larger win from writing is passing down things that are not commonly needed. If you hunt antelope every year I can teach my kids. If we know there are antelope "over there", but they are easy to over hunt to so we only hunt in 100 year droughts - nobody in the village will know how to hunt them when we need to and so we need writing.
